Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(December 2025)* |  *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/kæmˈbreɪ, kɒ̃ˈ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-MW-4) French: [[kɑ̃bʁɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Paris--Cambrai.ogg "File:Fr-Paris--Cambrai.ogg"); [Picard](/wiki/Picard_language "Picard language"): *Kimbré*; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Kamerijk*), formerly **Cambray**[[4]](#cite_note-MW-4) and historically in English **Camerick** or **Camericke**, is a city in the [Nord](/wiki/Nord_(French_department) "Nord (French department)") [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") and in the [Hauts-de-France](/wiki/Hauts-de-France "Hauts-de-France") [region](/wiki/Regions_of_France "Regions of France") of [France](/wiki/France "France") on the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") river, which is known locally as the Escaut river.

**Cambrai** ([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/kæmˈbreɪ, kɒ̃ˈ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-MW-4) French: [[kɑ̃bʁɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Paris--Cambrai.ogg "File:Fr-Paris--Cambrai.ogg"); [Picard](/wiki/Picard_language "Picard language"): *Kimbré*; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Kamerijk*), formerly **Cambray**[[4]](#cite_note-MW-4) and historically in English **Camerick** or **Camericke**, is a city in the [Nord](/wiki/Nord_(French_department) "Nord (French department)") [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") and in the [Hauts-de-France](/wiki/Hauts-de-France "Hauts-de-France") [region](/wiki/Regions_of_France "Regions of France") of [France](/wiki/France "France") on the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") river, which is known locally as the Escaut river.

A [sub-prefecture](/wiki/Subprefectures_in_France "Subprefectures in France") of the department, Cambrai is a town with about 31,000 inhabitants. It is in the heart of the [urban unit](/wiki/Urban_unit "Urban unit") of Cambrai with 46,000 inhabitants, and a [functional area](/wiki/Functional_area_(France) "Functional area (France)") with 93,000 inhabitants.[[5]](#cite_note-compar-5) With [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") and the towns of the former [Nord-Pas de Calais Mining Basin](/wiki/Nord-Pas_de_Calais_Mining_Basin "Nord-Pas de Calais Mining Basin"), it is also part of the [Metropolitan area of Lille](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_area_of_Lille&action=edit&redlink=1 "Metropolitan area of Lille (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aire_m%C3%A9tropolitaine_de_Lille "fr:Aire métropolitaine de Lille")] which has more than 3.8 million inhabitants.

Towards the end of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), Cambrai replaced [Bavay](/wiki/Bavay "Bavay") as the "capital" of the land of the [Nervii](/wiki/Nervii "Nervii"). At the beginning of the [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty") era, Cambrai became the seat of an immense [archdiocese](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Cambrai "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Cambrai") covering all the right bank of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") and the centre of a small [ecclesiastical principality](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_principality "Ecclesiastical principality"), roughly coinciding with the shire of [Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), including the central part of the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"). The bishopric had some limited secular power and depended on the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") until annexation to France in 1678. [Fénelon](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_F%C3%A9nelon "François Fénelon"), nicknamed the "Swan of Cambrai", was the most renowned of the archbishops.

The fertile lands which surround it and the textile industry gave it prosperity in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), but in modern times it is less industrialised than its neighbours of [Nord-Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Nord-Pas-de-Calais "Nord-Pas-de-Calais").

Cambrai was the [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Duke_of_Wellington "Duke of Wellington")'s headquarters, for the British Army of Occupation, from 1815 to 1818. Occupied by the German army during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Cambrai suffered partial destruction in the First Battle of Cambrai from British artillery attacks on the town, including the nearby [Bourlon](/wiki/Bourlon "Bourlon") Wood. The fighting around Cambrai, known as the [Battle of Cambrai](/wiki/Battle_of_Cambrai_(1917) "Battle of Cambrai (1917)") (20 November 1917 – 3 December 1917) is notable in that it is considered to be the first mass use of [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") in battle.[[6]](#cite_note-6) A second [Battle of Cambrai](/wiki/Battle_of_Cambrai_(1918) "Battle of Cambrai (1918)") took place between 8 and 10 October 1918 as part of the [Hundred Days Offensive](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive "Hundred Days Offensive"). [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") was followed by reconstructions and a rapidly developing economy and population, abruptly reversed by the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis").

Cambrai today is a lively city and, despite the past destruction, maintains a rich monumental heritage. Cambrai is affirmed as the urban centre of [Cambrésis](/wiki/Cambr%C3%A9sis "Cambrésis"). Its economic life is strengthened by its position on the main local highway and river.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Peutinger_Casaromago.jpg/250px-Peutinger_Casaromago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peutinger_Casaromago.jpg)

An extract from the Peutinger table showing Camaraco (Cambrai) northeast of Sammarobriva (present-day [Amiens](/wiki/Amiens "Amiens"))

Little is known with certainty of the beginnings of Cambrai. *Camaracum* or *Camaraco*, as it was known to the Romans, is mentioned for the first time on the [Peutinger table](/wiki/Tabula_Peutingeriana "Tabula Peutingeriana") in the middle of the 4th century. It became the main town of the Roman province of the [Nervii](/wiki/Nervii "Nervii"), whose first Roman capital had been at *Bagacum*, present-day [Bavay](/wiki/Bavay "Bavay").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the middle of the 4th-century, [Frankish](/wiki/Franks "Franks") raids from the north threatened Bavay and led the Romans to build forts along the Cologne to Bavay to Cambrai road, and thence to Boulogne. Cambrai thus occupied an important strategic position.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 430, the [Salian Franks](/wiki/Salian_Franks "Salian Franks") under the command of [Clodio](/wiki/Clodio "Clodio") the Long-Haired took the town. In 509, [Clovis](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I") undertook to unify the Frankish kingdoms[[b 1]](#cite_note-7) by getting rid of his relatives.

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

Cambrai began to grow from a rural market into a real city during the [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian "Merovingian") times, a long period of peace when the bishoprics of Arras and Cambrai were first unified (probably owing to the small number of clerics left at the time) and were later transferred to Cambrai, an administrative centre for the region. Successive bishops, including [Gaugericus](/wiki/Gaugericus "Gaugericus") (in French Géry), founded abbeys and churches to host relics, which contributed powerfully to giving Cambrai both the appearance and functions of a city.[[b 2]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Trait%C3%A9_de_Verdun_843.png/250px-Trait%C3%A9_de_Verdun_843.png)](/wiki/File:Trait%C3%A9_de_Verdun_843.png)

Following the [Treaty of Verdun](/wiki/Treaty_of_Verdun "Treaty of Verdun"), Cambrai found itself as a "median" border city of the kingdom of Lothair I with the western lands of Charles the Bald

When the [Treaty of Verdun](/wiki/Treaty_of_Verdun "Treaty of Verdun") in 843 split [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne")'s empire into three parts, the county of Cambrai fell into [Lothaire](/wiki/Lothair_I "Lothair I")'s kingdom. However, upon the death of [Lothair II](/wiki/Lothair_II "Lothair II"), who had no heir, king [Charles the Bald](/wiki/Charles_the_Bald "Charles the Bald") tried to gain control of his kingdom by having himself consecrated at [Metz](/wiki/Metz "Metz"). Cambrai thus reverted, but only briefly, to the [Western Frankish Realm](/wiki/West_Francia "West Francia"). In 870 the town was destroyed by the Normans.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Beffroi_de_Cambrai_vu_de_la_Rue_du_Beffroi.jpg/250px-Beffroi_de_Cambrai_vu_de_la_Rue_du_Beffroi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beffroi_de_Cambrai_vu_de_la_Rue_du_Beffroi.jpg)

The belfry of Cambrai, the old bell tower of the Church of Saint Martin, symbol of communal freedoms

In the Middle Ages the region around Cambrai, called Cambrésis, was a county. Rivalries between the count, who ruled the city and county, and the bishop, ceased when in 948 [Otto I](/wiki/Otto_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Otto I, Holy Roman Emperor") granted the [bishop](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Cambrai "Archdiocese of Cambrai") with temporal powers over the city.[[b 3]](#cite_note-10) In 1007, Emperor [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry II, Holy Roman Emperor") extended the bishop's temporal power to the territory surrounding Cambrai. The bishops then had both spiritual and temporal powers. This made Cambrai and Cambrésis a church principality, much like [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"), an independent state which was part of the Holy Roman Empire. The spiritual power of the bishop was exercised over a vast diocese, which stretched on the right bank of the Scheldt to [Mons](/wiki/Mons,_Belgium "Mons, Belgium"), Brussels and Antwerp.[[8]](#cite_note-11) In 954, the [Magyars](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") under [Bulcsú](/wiki/Bulcs%C3%BA_(chieftain) "Bulcsú (chieftain)") besieged Cambrai, which resisted all their attacks.[[9]](#cite_note-12)

In 958 one of the first [communal](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") uprisings in Europe occurred in Cambrai. The inhabitants rebelled against Bishop Bérenger's power and abuses. The rebellion was severely repressed, but the discontent flared up again in the 10th and 11th centuries. Between 1077 and 1215, the [burghers](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") had a charter franchise on at least four occasions. Each time, these were eventually withdrawn by the combined efforts of the bishops and emperors. In 1227, following another period of unrest, the burghers of Cambrai finally had to give up their charters and accept the bishop's authority. However, the *Loi Godefroid* promulgated by the bishop, in fact or in law, left the people a number of freedoms won in the management of communal affairs.[[10]](#cite_note-13)

Cambrai is also known for its Irish [homily](/wiki/Cambrai_Homily "Cambrai Homily").

#### Economic activity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Economic activity")]

[![Plan of Cambrai drawn in 1649](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Cambrai_-_Cameracvm_vulgo_Cambray_-_Kamerijk_%28Atlas_van_Loon%29.jpg/250px-Cambrai_-_Cameracvm_vulgo_Cambray_-_Kamerijk_%28Atlas_van_Loon%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambrai_-_Cameracvm_vulgo_Cambray_-_Kamerijk_(Atlas_van_Loon).jpg)

Plan of Cambrai drawn in 1649, depicting the outline of the 11th century walls

In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the city grew richer and larger thanks to its weaving industry which produced woollen cloth, linen and [cambric](/wiki/Cambric "Cambric"). Cambrai, and in particular the drapery, experienced an economic decline from the 15th century.[[b 4]](#cite_note-p.98-14) Cambrai then belonged to a commercial [hansa](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") of seventeen low country cities whose aim was to develop trade with the fairs in [Champagne](/wiki/Champagne,_France "Champagne, France") and [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"). By the 11th century, the city walls had reached the circumference they would keep until the 19th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Music history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Music history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/DufayBinchois.jpg/250px-DufayBinchois.jpg)](/wiki/File:DufayBinchois.jpg)

Dufay (left) in conversation with [Gilles Binchois](/wiki/Gilles_Binchois "Gilles Binchois")

Cambrai has a distinguished musical history, particularly in the 15th century. The [cathedral](/wiki/Old_Cambrai_Cathedral "Old Cambrai Cathedral") there, a musical centre until the 17th century, had one of the most active musical establishments in the Low Countries; many composers of the [Burgundian School](/wiki/Burgundian_School "Burgundian School") either grew up and learned their craft there, or returned to teach. In 1428, Philippe de Luxembourg claimed that the cathedral was the finest in all of Christianity, for the fineness of its singing, its light, and the sweetness of its bells. [Guillaume Dufay](/wiki/Guillaume_Dufay "Guillaume Dufay"), the most famous European musician of the 15th century, studied at the cathedral from 1409 to 1412 under Nicolas Malin and [Richard Loqueville](/wiki/Richard_Loqueville "Richard Loqueville"), and returned in 1439 after spending many years in Italy. Cambrai cathedral had other famous composers in the later 15th century: [Johannes Tinctoris](/wiki/Johannes_Tinctoris "Johannes Tinctoris") and [Ockeghem](/wiki/Johannes_Ockeghem "Johannes Ockeghem") went to Cambrai to study with Dufay.[[b 5]](#cite_note-15) Other composers included [Nicolas Grenon](/wiki/Nicolas_Grenon "Nicolas Grenon"), [Alexander Agricola](/wiki/Alexander_Agricola "Alexander Agricola"), and [Jacob Obrecht](/wiki/Jacob_Obrecht "Jacob Obrecht"). In the 16th century, [Philippe de Monte](/wiki/Philippe_de_Monte "Philippe de Monte"), [Johannes Lupi](/wiki/Johannes_Lupi "Johannes Lupi"), and [Jacobus de Kerle](/wiki/Jacobus_de_Kerle "Jacobus de Kerle") all worked there.

### Early Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early Modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Maison_des_Canonniers_et_Maison_de_l%27Arbrisseau_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg/250px-Maison_des_Canonniers_et_Maison_de_l%27Arbrisseau_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maison_des_Canonniers_et_Maison_de_l%27Arbrisseau_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg)

The "gunners' house" in Cambrai is an example of 17th-century Flemish architecture

[![Plan of Cambrai in 1710](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Map_of_Cambrai_1710.jpg/250px-Map_of_Cambrai_1710.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Cambrai_1710.jpg)

Plan of Cambrai in 1710

As the economic centre of northern Europe moved away from [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), the area became poorer, with an associated period of cultural decline.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, the city's neutrality and its position between the possessions of the [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") and [France](/wiki/France "France") made it the venue of several international negotiations, including the [League of Cambrai](/wiki/League_of_Cambrai "League of Cambrai"), an alliance engineered in 1508 by [Pope Julius II](/wiki/Pope_Julius_II "Pope Julius II") against the [Republic of Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice"), concluding in the [Treaty of Cambrai](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cambrai_(1508) "Treaty of Cambrai (1508)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trait%C3%A9_de_Cambrai "fr:Traité de Cambrai")].[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The alliance collapsed in 1510 when the Pope allied with Venice against his former ally [France](/wiki/France "France"). The conflict is also referred to as the [War of the League of Cambrai](/wiki/War_of_the_League_of_Cambrai "War of the League of Cambrai") and lasted from 1508 to 1516. Cambrai was also the site of negotiations in 1529, concluding in the [Paix des Dames](/wiki/Paix_des_Dames "Paix des Dames"), which led to France's withdrawal from the [War of the League of Cognac](/wiki/War_of_the_League_of_Cognac "War of the League of Cognac").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1595, the city was taken by the Spanish in the eighth and last [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

In December 1623, the community of nuns of the [English Benedictine Congregation](/wiki/English_Benedictine_Congregation "English Benedictine Congregation") was founded at Cambrai.[[11]](#cite_note-16) Expelled in 1793 as a result of the French Revolution, its successor community in 1838 was [Stanbrook Abbey](/wiki/Stanbrook_Abbey "Stanbrook Abbey"), near [Malvern](/wiki/Malvern,_Worcestershire "Malvern, Worcestershire") and later [Wass](/wiki/Wass,_North_Yorkshire "Wass, North Yorkshire") in Yorkshire.

In 1630, [Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu"), wishing to counter the power of the Emperor and Spain, renewed the alliance of France with the [United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"). The main effort of France had to focus on the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"), and a sharing plan was established with the Dutch, with France to receive the Hainaut, Cambrésis, Artois, a large part of Flanders and Luxembourg and the County of Namur.[[12]](#cite_note-17) [War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War#French_intervention_and_continued_Swedish_participation_(1635–1648) "Thirty Years' War") was declared against Spain in 1635: It was followed by a [long series of wars](/wiki/Franco-Spanish_War_(1635%E2%80%9359) "Franco-Spanish War (1635–59)") which, compounded by subsistence and epidemics, caused crises which would bruise the [Cambrésis](/wiki/Cambr%C3%A9sis "Cambrésis").[[b 6]](#cite_note-Trenard145-18)

[Mazarin](/wiki/Cardinal_Mazarin "Cardinal Mazarin") tried unsuccessfully, in 1649, to seize the city, which was being besieged by [Henri de Lorraine-Harcourt](/wiki/Henri,_Count_of_Harcourt "Henri, Count of Harcourt") and the [Vicomte de Turenne](/w/index.php?title=Henri_de_la_Tour_d%27Auvergne,_Viscount_of_Turenne&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henri de la Tour d'Auvergne, Viscount of Turenne (page does not exist)"). A Spanish regiment, which came from [Bouchain](/wiki/Bouchain "Bouchain"), succeeded in entering the city and the siege was lifted. In 1657, the Vicomte de Turenne captured Cambrai. Again 4,000 horsemen under the command of [Condé](/wiki/Louis,_Grand_Cond%C3%A9 "Louis, Grand Condé"), in the service of the Spain, manage to penetrate, and Turenne abandoned the city.[[b 7]](#cite_note-19)

In 1666, in the greatest secrecy, [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") prepared new conquests by making plans of the Spanish fortifications, and then began the [War of Devolution](/wiki/War_of_Devolution "War of Devolution"). The [Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aix-la-Chapelle_(1668) "Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1668)") from 1668 allowed the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") to obtain a large number of strongholds, but Cambrai was not a part of them, nor were [Bouchain](/wiki/Bouchain "Bouchain"), [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes") or [Condé-sur-l'Escaut](/wiki/Cond%C3%A9-sur-l%27Escaut "Condé-sur-l'Escaut").

#### The annexation by France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The annexation by France")]

In 1672, [hostilities](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War") resumed against the Protestant Republic of the Netherlands and continued in the following years. In 1676, [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), in an effort to "safeguard the tranquility of his borders for ever" ("*assurer à jamais le repos de ses frontières*"), focused most of his efforts against Spain and occupied [Condé](/wiki/Cond%C3%A9-sur-l%27Escaut "Condé-sur-l'Escaut") and [Bouchain](/wiki/Bouchain "Bouchain"). On 17 March 1677, the French troops [stormed Valenciennes](/wiki/Siege_of_Valenciennes_(1676%E2%80%9377) "Siege of Valenciennes (1676–77)") and moved toward Cambrai, the strongest place of the Netherlands,[[b 8]](#cite_note-20) which was reached on 20 March.[[13]](#cite_note-21) On 22 March, Louis XIV was in the city in person.[[b 9]](#cite_note-22) On 2 April, the French invested in a part of the place. By 5 April, the city surrendered, given the same benefits as Lille in 1667,[[b 10]](#cite_note-p.150-23) but the Spanish garrison took refuge in the [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel") and the siege continued until 17 April. After [29 days of siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Cambrai_(1677) "Siege of Cambrai (1677)") the king made his entry into the city, on 19 April, Easter Monday.[[b 11]](#cite_note-24) Louis XIV named the Marquis de Cesen as governor, and appointed 14 new [aldermen](/wiki/Alderman "Alderman") while keeping the same [provost](/wiki/Pr%C3%A9v%C3%B4t "Prévôt"). The [Hôtel de Ville](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Cambrai "Hôtel de Ville, Cambrai") (town hall), in its current style, dates from 1786.[[14]](#cite_note-25)

### The French Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The French Revolution")]

The city suffered from the [Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"): [Joseph Le Bon](/wiki/Joseph_Le_Bon "Joseph Le Bon"), sent by the [Comité de salut public](/wiki/Comit%C3%A9_de_salut_public "Comité de salut public"), arrived in Cambrai in 1794.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He was to set up an era of "terror", sending many to the [guillotine](/wiki/Guillotine "Guillotine"), until he was tried and executed in 1795.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] One of his most famous victims was [François III Maximilien de la Woestyne, 3rd Marquess of Becelaere](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_III_Maximilien_de_la_Woestyne,_3rd_Marquess_of_Becelaere "François III Maximilien de la Woestyne, 3rd Marquess of Becelaere").

Most of the religious buildings of the city were demolished in that period: in 1797, the [old cathedral](/wiki/Old_Cambrai_Cathedral "Old Cambrai Cathedral"), which had been dubbed the "wonder of the low countries", was sold to a merchant on 6 June 1796 who left only the tower,[[d 1]](#cite_note-26) after exploiting the cathedral as a stone quarry. The main tower was left standing until 1809, when it collapsed[[d 2]](#cite_note-27) in a storm. However the cathedral's archives have been preserved (they are now at the Archives Départementales du Nord in [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille")) and a [new cathedral](/wiki/Cambrai_Cathedral "Cambrai Cathedral") was later provided.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Boulevard_Faidherbe_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg/250px-Boulevard_Faidherbe_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boulevard_Faidherbe_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg)

The Boulevard Faidherbe, built in 1898 on the site of the ramparts

The [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") of 1870 widely spared Cambrai. It also showed the futility of the fortifications, which the city obtained permission to demolish, at its expense, in 1892.[[b 12]](#cite_note-28) The outer boulevards were constructed on the location of the walls, between 1894 and the beginning of the 20th century. The appearance of the city was radically transformed, and the works stimulated the city's economy.[[b 13]](#cite_note-29)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/LL_16_-_Cambrai_-_Vue_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_vers_la_Place_d%27Armes.JPG/250px-LL_16_-_Cambrai_-_Vue_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_vers_la_Place_d%27Armes.JPG)](/wiki/File:LL_16_-_Cambrai_-_Vue_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_vers_la_Place_d%27Armes.JPG)

The *Place d'Armes*, on a market day before the First World War

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1974-029-09%2C_Frankreich%2C_Cambrai%2C_Fl%C3%BCchtlinge.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1974-029-09%2C_Frankreich%2C_Cambrai%2C_Fl%C3%BCchtlinge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1974-029-09,_Frankreich,_Cambrai,_Fl%C3%BCchtlinge.jpg)

Refugees at Cambrai in September 1918

In 1914, the German army occupied the city. This occupation, which lasted for four years, was marked by scenes of looting, requisitions and arrests of hostages. From 20 November to 17 December 1917, the vicinity of the town of Cambrai was the theatre of the [Battle of Cambrai](/wiki/Battle_of_Cambrai_(1917) "Battle of Cambrai (1917)"), which saw the massed use of [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") for the first time.

In 1918, the Germans burned the city centre before leaving, destroying the city hall and the municipal archives. In total, more than 1,500 buildings were totally destroyed, of the 3,500 which consisted of Cambrai. The centre was to be rebuilt, a task which was entrusted to the architect [Pierre Leprince-Ringuet](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_Leprince-Ringuet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre Leprince-Ringuet (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierre_Leprince-Ringuet "fr:Pierre Leprince-Ringuet")].[[b 14]](#cite_note-30)[[b 15]](#cite_note-31)

[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") also struck Cambrai. The city was bombed by the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") on 17 May 1940, during the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"), before falling the next day at the same time as [Saint-Quentin](/wiki/Saint-Quentin,_Aisne "Saint-Quentin, Aisne"). The remains of the [9th French Army](/wiki/Ninth_Army_(France) "Ninth Army (France)") and [General Giraud](/wiki/Henri_Giraud "Henri Giraud") were taken prisoner by the Germans.[[15]](#cite_note-32)[[16]](#cite_note-33)

From 27 April until 18 August 1944, 18 [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") air raids were directed against the railway tracks, killing 250 people and destroying 1,700 buildings,[[b 16]](#cite_note-34) or more than 50% of the city.[[17]](#cite_note-35) The first American tanks entered the city on 2 September.

After the war, the priority again was reconstruction. A municipality of the "union of the left" was elected in 1945, led by [Raymond Gernez](/w/index.php?title=Raymond_Gernez&action=edit&redlink=1 "Raymond Gernez (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raymond_Gernez "fr:Raymond Gernez")] who would remain at the head of the city until 1981, promoting moderate socialism.[[b 17]](#cite_note-36) As early as 1947, the city submitted to a development project of the Ministry of Reconstruction. The municipality gave priority to the construction of houses: The *Maison du Cambrésis* [House of le Cambrésis], later *Maison Familiale* group, an [HLM](/wiki/HLM "HLM") cooperative society, contributed substantially to the reconstruction of the city.[[b 18]](#cite_note-37) The population of the city increased, while the surrounding area tended to be depopulated. At the same time, the city lost industrial jobs and moved towards the tertiary sector, but it was public administrations which provided the bulk of jobs.[[b 19]](#cite_note-38)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Cambrai-Position.png/250px-Cambrai-Position.png)](/wiki/File:Cambrai-Position.png)

Geographic situation of Cambrai in the Nord department

The town of Cambrai is situated in the southern part of the Nord Department, of which it is [chef-lieu](/wiki/Chef-lieu "Chef-lieu") of the [arrondissement](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Cambrai "Arrondissement of Cambrai"). It belongs to the dense network of the cities of the area which are separated by a few tens of kilometres: [Douai](/wiki/Douai "Douai") is only 24 kilometres (15 mi) away, [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes") is 29 kilometres (18 mi), [Arras](/wiki/Arras "Arras") is 35 kilometres (22 mi) and [Saint-Quentin](/wiki/Saint-Quentin,_Aisne "Saint-Quentin, Aisne") 37 kilometres (23 mi), all measured [as the crow flies](/wiki/As_the_crow_flies "As the crow flies").[[18]](#cite_note-39) The regional capital, [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille"), is 52 kilometres (32 mi) from Cambrai.

Cambrai is not very far from several European capitals: [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") is 108 kilometres (67 mi) away, [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") is 160 kilometres (99 mi) and [London](/wiki/London "London") is 279 kilometres (173 mi).

The city was born and developed on the right bank of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") river, locally known as the Escaut. The river has its source in the department of [Aisne](/wiki/Aisne "Aisne"), just a little over 20 kilometres (12 mi) away.

### Geology and landforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geology and landforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/NPdC_relief_Cambrai.png/250px-NPdC_relief_Cambrai.png)](/wiki/File:NPdC_relief_Cambrai.png)

Cambrai in its topographical context

Cambrai is located on [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") bedrock of the [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") period[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], which forms the northern boundary of the [Paris Basin](/wiki/Paris_Basin "Paris Basin"), between, to the east, the hills for [Thiérache](/wiki/Thi%C3%A9rache "Thiérache") and [Avesnois](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Avesnes-sur-Helpe "Arrondissement of Avesnes-sur-Helpe"), the foothills of the [Ardennes](/w/index.php?title=Massif_ardennais&action=edit&redlink=1 "Massif ardennais (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massif_ardennais "fr:Massif ardennais")], and northwest, the hills of [Artois](/wiki/Artois "Artois"). It is at a point which is relatively lower than these two regions, called the "Cambrai threshold" or the "Bapaume threshold", which facilitates the passage between the south and the north: Bapaume (Artois) is 100 metres (330 ft) above sea level, [Avesnes-sur-Helpe](/wiki/Avesnes-sur-Helpe "Avesnes-sur-Helpe") (Avesnois) is at 143 metres (469 ft) and Cambrai only 41 metres (135 ft). The [Saint-Quentin canal](/wiki/Canal_de_Saint-Quentin "Canal de Saint-Quentin"), the [Canal du Nord](/wiki/Canal_du_Nord "Canal du Nord"), the [A1](/wiki/A1_autoroute_(France) "A1 autoroute (France)"), [A2](/wiki/A2_autoroute_(France) "A2 autoroute (France)") and [A26](/wiki/A26_autoroute "A26 autoroute") autoroutes all borrow all this passage between the basin of the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") and the plains of the Nord department.

The chalky subsoil allowed, as in many medieval cities, the digging of a network of cellars, tunnels and quarries under the city. The poor quality of the Cambrai chalk was reserved for use in the manufacture of lime or filling, as well as common constructions. For prestigious buildings, stone from the nearby villages of [Noyelles-sur-Escaut](/wiki/Noyelles-sur-Escaut "Noyelles-sur-Escaut"), [Rumilly](/wiki/Rumilly-en-Cambr%C3%A9sis "Rumilly-en-Cambrésis") or [Marcoing](/wiki/Marcoing "Marcoing") was used.[[a 1]](#cite_note-p.37-40)

The city is bordered in its western part, as well as to the north and the south, by the alluvial zones of the Scheldt Valley.

### Hydrography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hydrography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Tour_des_Arquets_Cambrai_2.JPG/250px-Tour_des_Arquets_Cambrai_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tour_des_Arquets_Cambrai_2.JPG)

The Tower of Arquets (14th century) controlled the entry of the Scheldt in the city and the flood defences.

Cambrai is built on the right bank of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"). The river, still of a very modest flow in Cambrai,[[19]](#cite_note-41) played a crucial role in the history of the city by providing multiple functions, including allowing the transportation of men and goods since antiquity. However, it was undeveloped and was crossed by numerous marshes. It was with the discovery of coal at [Anzin](/wiki/Anzin "Anzin") in 1734 that the Scheldt was expanded and declared navigable in 1780, from Cambrai to the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").[[a 2]](#cite_note-42) The Scheldt is today the [Canal de l'Escaut](/wiki/Canal_de_l%27Escaut "Canal de l'Escaut") downstream of Cambrai.

In addition, the river initially served as the boundary between the bishoprics of [Tournai](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Tournai "Roman Catholic Diocese of Tournai") on its left bank and [Cambrai](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Cambrai "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Cambrai") on its right bank, from the 6th century.[[c 1]](#cite_note-43) When the division of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne")'s Empire in 843, this border was retained to delimit the kingdoms of [Lothair I](/wiki/Lothair_I "Lothair I") and [Charles the Bald](/wiki/Charles_the_Bald "Charles the Bald"), making Cambrai a city of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") until 1677.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Scheldt was also indispensable to many economic activities, such as the tanning, milling, the manufacture of salt and soap,[[a 3]](#cite_note-p.62-44) as well as for [retting](/wiki/Retting "Retting") of [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen"), the weaving of which was one of the main activities of the city.[[b 4]](#cite_note-p.98-14)

Finally, the river was used in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and then by [Vauban](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Le_Prestre_de_Vauban "Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban"), for the defence of the city by the establishment of flood defensive areas.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Despite its important role in the history of the city, the Scheldt is little integrated into the present urban landscape.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Climate")]

:   Main article: *[Climate of Nord-Pas-de-Calais](/w/index.php?title=Climate_of_Nord-Pas-de-Calais&action=edit&redlink=1 "Climate of Nord-Pas-de-Calais (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climat_du_Nord-Pas-de-Calais "fr:Climat du Nord-Pas-de-Calais")]*

Climate in this area has mild differences between highs and lows, and there is adequate rainfall year round. The [Köppen Climate Classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Classification "Köppen Climate Classification") subtype for this climate is "[Cfb](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification#Group_C:_Temperate/mesothermal_climates "Köppen climate classification")" (Marine West Coast Climate/[Oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate")).[[20]](#cite_note-45) However, the city is about 110 kilometres (68 mi) from the nearest coast.[[note 1]](#cite_note-46)

[Precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation") is distributed year-round, with highs in the spring and autumn, with February being the driest month. Contrasting with the rainy image of the region, the total annual precipitation is relatively small with 642 millimetres (25.3 in) at Cambrai-Épinoy; identical to the Montsouris Paris station, which is at the same altitude, it is less than those of [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse") at 656 millimetres (25.8 in) or [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") at 767 millimetres (30.2 in). However, the number of days of rain (63 in Nice, 120 in Cambrai) confirms the oceanic character of the climate.

The mean [thermal amplitude](/wiki/Atmospheric_temperature_range "Atmospheric temperature range") between the winter and summer does not exceed 15 °C. Although again establishing a comparison with Paris, that Cambrai is 1.5 to 2 °C colder over all combined seasons.[[21]](#cite_note-47) On average, there are 71 days of fog per year (Paris-Montsouris has 13) 15 days of storm (Paris-Montsouris has 19) and 20 days with snow (Paris-Montsouris has 15).

If comparing the data of Cambrai and those of towns such as [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk") or [Boulogne](/wiki/Boulogne-sur-Mer "Boulogne-sur-Mer"),[[22]](#cite_note-48) there are colder minimum temperatures and a warmer maximum in Cambrai, the difference being approximately 2 °C, as well as a larger number of freezing days and less heavy precipitation: It's described a "transitional" oceanic climate, with some continental influences.

The temperature record in Cambrai is 38.2 °C (100.8 °F), which was established on 6 August 2003[[23]](#cite_note-49) (data collected since 1954 and record updated to 5 September 2013).

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Road")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/French_A26_motorway.jpg/250px-French_A26_motorway.jpg)](/wiki/File:French_A26_motorway.jpg)

The A26 autoroute at its intersection with the A2, near Cambrai

Cambrai is located at the crossroads of two French autoroutes, the [A2](/wiki/A2_autoroute_(France) "A2 autoroute (France)") from [Combles](/wiki/Combles "Combles") (junction with the [A1](/wiki/A1_autoroute_(France) "A1 autoroute (France)") coming from Paris) to the Franco-Belgian border, opened in 1973, and the [A26](/wiki/A26_autoroute "A26 autoroute") from [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais") to [Troyes](/wiki/Troyes "Troyes"), opened in 1992. These autoroutes partly merge with the [European roads](/wiki/International_E-road_network "International E-road network") of the [E19](/wiki/European_route_E19 "European route E19") from [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") via [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), for the A2, and the [E17](/wiki/European_route_E17 "European route E17") from [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") to [Beaune](/wiki/Beaune "Beaune") via [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") and [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims"), for the A26.

Cambrai and its region are served by four autoroute interchanges: The A2, exit 14 (Cambrai) from [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") and exit 15 ([Bouchain](/wiki/Bouchain "Bouchain")) from [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), and on the A26 the exits 8 ([Marquion](/wiki/Marquion "Marquion")) from [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais") and 9 ([Masnières](/wiki/Masni%C3%A8res "Masnières")) from [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims").

Cambrai is also at the crossroads of the [Route nationale 30](/wiki/Route_nationale_30 "Route nationale 30") of [Bapaume](/wiki/Bapaume "Bapaume") to [Quiévrain](/wiki/Qui%C3%A9vrain "Quiévrain") (Franco-Belgian border), [Route nationale 43](/wiki/Route_nationale_43 "Route nationale 43") of [Sainte-Ruffine](/wiki/Sainte-Ruffine "Sainte-Ruffine") ([Metz](/wiki/Metz "Metz")) to [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais"), [Route nationale 44](/wiki/Route_nationale_44 "Route nationale 44") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Route_nationale_44_(France) "fr:Route nationale 44 (France)")] of Cambrai to [Vitry](/wiki/Vitry-le-Fran%C3%A7ois "Vitry-le-François") (these last three have since 2006 been downgraded to [departmental roads](/w/index.php?title=French_departmental_road_network&action=edit&redlink=1 "French departmental road network (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_routier_d%C3%A9partemental_fran%C3%A7ais "fr:Réseau routier départemental français")] and therefore consequently renamed to D6xx), and D939 (former [Route nationale 39](/wiki/Route_nationale_39 "Route nationale 39") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Route_nationale_39_(France) "fr:Route nationale 39 (France)")]) of Cambrai to [Arras](/wiki/Arras "Arras").

To facilitate access to the east of the [Cambrésis](/wiki/Cambr%C3%A9sis "Cambrésis") from the A2 and A26 autoroutes, to alleviate traffic in the crossing of the city and to serve the future Niergnies business zone, a southern bypass was the subject of a [declaration of public utility](/wiki/D%C3%A9claration_d%27utilit%C3%A9_publique "Déclaration d'utilité publique") (DPU) on 22 April 1999. Its route has been repeatedly modified and challenged, because it crosses the urban ecological park of the [Chenu Wood](/w/index.php?title=Regional_Natural_Reserve_of_the_River_Escaut&action=edit&redlink=1 "Regional Natural Reserve of the River Escaut (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/R%C3%A9serve_naturelle_r%C3%A9gionale_de_l%27Escaut-rivi%C3%A8re "fr:Réserve naturelle régionale de l'Escaut-rivière")] in [Proville](/wiki/Proville "Proville"),[[25]](#cite_note-51) the only public natural green space of the Cambrésis. The bypass is in service since 17 September 2010.[[26]](#cite_note-VdNContSud-52)

A bypass to the north is also part of the program of major departmental projects, which was required to have been initiated by 2011.[[26]](#cite_note-VdNContSud-52)

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Railway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Gare_de_Cambrai.JPG/250px-Gare_de_Cambrai.JPG)](/wiki/File:Gare_de_Cambrai.JPG)

The SNCF railway station in Cambrai

[Cambrai railway station](/wiki/Gare_de_Cambrai "Gare de Cambrai") is connected by direct trains ([TER](/wiki/TER_Hauts-de-France "TER Hauts-de-France")) to [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille"), [Douai](/wiki/Douai "Douai"), [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes"), [Saint-Quentin](/wiki/Saint-Quentin,_Aisne "Saint-Quentin, Aisne"), and [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims").

The link to Douai and Lille has improved after the electrification of the single-track [Douai to Cambrai line](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Just-en-Chauss%C3%A9e_to_Douai_railway_line&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saint-Just-en-Chaussée to Douai railway line (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ligne_de_Saint-Just-en-Chauss%C3%A9e_%C3%A0_Douai "fr:Ligne de Saint-Just-en-Chaussée à Douai")] in 1993, for the commissioning of joining [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") to Cambrai by [TGV](/wiki/TGV "TGV") via Douai, which was subsequently cancelled. Direct connections on weekdays are fifteen trains per day, with a journey time of about 30 minutes between Douai and Cambrai; eight trains, with a journey time often less than an hour, to [Lille-Flandres station](/wiki/Gare_de_Lille_Flandres "Gare de Lille Flandres"); ten trains, with a time of little more than 40 minutes on average to Valenciennes and a dozen trains, with a journey time of around 50 minutes, to Saint-Quentin.[[27]](#cite_note-sncf-53)

Links to Paris from Cambrai ([Gare du Nord](/wiki/Gare_du_Nord "Gare du Nord")) are mediocre if compared to those of the neighbouring cities. Valenciennes, Douai and Arras are connected to Paris by TGV several times per day. Saint-Quentin is connected by TER or [Intercités](/wiki/Intercit%C3%A9s "Intercités") trains with less than two hours travel time. Cambrai was connected to Paris in 2010 by a single direct Intercités service of two hours, with a little-suited schedule for professional use. The times of other trains via Douai or Saint-Quentin vary between two and four and a half hours.[[28]](#cite_note-54)

#### Railway history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Railway history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Gare_de_Cambrai_1913.jpg/250px-Gare_de_Cambrai_1913.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gare_de_Cambrai_1913.jpg)

The [Gare de Cambrai-Annexe](/w/index.php?title=Gare_de_Cambrai-Annexe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gare de Cambrai-Annexe (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gare_de_Cambrai-Annexe "fr:Gare de Cambrai-Annexe")] in 1913

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Gare_cambr%C3%A9sis_cambrai.jpg/250px-Gare_cambr%C3%A9sis_cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gare_cambr%C3%A9sis_cambrai.jpg)

The Gare du Cambrésis, former head of the line of the [Chemin de fer du Cambrésis](/wiki/Chemin_de_fer_du_Cambr%C3%A9sis "Chemin de fer du Cambrésis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Port_de_plaisance_Cantimpr%C3%A9_Cambrai.JPG/250px-Port_de_plaisance_Cantimpr%C3%A9_Cambrai.JPG)](/wiki/File:Port_de_plaisance_Cantimpr%C3%A9_Cambrai.JPG)

The marina of Cantimpré

As early as 1833, the municipal council sought passage of a railway line through Cambrai. However, a route through Arras and Douai, to Lille, with a branch to Valenciennes, was preferred in 1845. It remained, therefore, to connect Cambrai to this line, which was done in 1878 by a single, winding line between Cambrai and Douai. Meanwhile, Cambrai had been linked, in 1858, to the Paris-Brussels line by a branch line from [Busigny](/wiki/Busigny "Busigny") and joining [Somain](/wiki/Somain,_Nord "Somain, Nord"), near Douai.[[b 10]](#cite_note-p.150-23)

Other railway lines of [local interest](/wiki/Voie_ferr%C3%A9e_d%27int%C3%A9r%C3%AAt_local "Voie ferrée d'intérêt local") saw development in the 19th century, particularly in 1880 the [Société des Chemin de fer du Cambrésis](/wiki/Chemin_de_fer_du_Cambr%C3%A9sis "Chemin de fer du Cambrésis") which operated three routes in the Cambrésis between Cambrai, Caudry, Saint-Quentin, Le Cateau and Denain. An agricultural use of Cambrai to Marquion, now off line, was also open in 1898.[[a 1]](#cite_note-p.37-40)

Cambrai railway station was also the terminus of a standard gauge secondary route of [CGL/VFIL](/w/index.php?title=Compagnie_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_de_voies_ferr%C3%A9es_d%27int%C3%A9r%C3%AAt_local&action=edit&redlink=1 "Compagnie générale de voies ferrées d'intérêt local (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compagnie_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_de_voies_ferr%C3%A9es_d%27int%C3%A9r%C3%AAt_local "fr:Compagnie générale de voies ferrées d'intérêt local")] linking [Marquion](/wiki/Marquion "Marquion") and [Boisleux-au-Mont](/wiki/Boisleux-au-Mont "Boisleux-au-Mont").

#### Railway projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Railway projects")]

The regional transport scheme evokes three axes or projects concerning Cambrai: Improving the Douai-Cambrai connections, the construction of a new railway line between Cambrai and [Marquion](/wiki/Marquion "Marquion")-[Arras](/wiki/Arras "Arras"), in conjunction with the large gauge [Seine-North Europe canal](/wiki/Seine%E2%80%93Nord_Europe_Canal "Seine–Nord Europe Canal") project and the establishment of a business zone of Marquion, as well as "searching for a link from [Orchies](/wiki/Orchies "Orchies") towards Cambrai".[[29]](#cite_note-55)

### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Waterways")]

Cambrai is one of the seven territorial subdivisions of the regional management of Nord-Pas-de-Calais of [navigable waterways in France](/wiki/Voies_navigables_de_France "Voies navigables de France"). The city is located at the junction of the [Saint-Quentin canal](/wiki/Canal_de_Saint-Quentin "Canal de Saint-Quentin") to the Oise and Paris and the [Canal de l'Escaut](/wiki/Canal_de_l%27Escaut "Canal de l'Escaut"), which leads to the [Dunkerque-Escaut canal](/wiki/Canal_Dunkerque-Escaut "Canal Dunkerque-Escaut").
A [marina](/wiki/Marina "Marina") is located at the junction of the two canals, at Cambrai-Cantimpré.

#### Waterways history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Waterways history")]

The [Canal de l'Escaut](/wiki/Canal_de_l%27Escaut "Canal de l'Escaut") ([Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") canal), between Cambrai and [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes"), was opened to navigation in 1780.

Moreover, a river link between Paris and the Nord department had been projected as early as the time of [Mazarin](/wiki/Cardinal_Mazarin "Cardinal Mazarin") and [Colbert](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert "Jean-Baptiste Colbert"). The construction of the [Saint-Quentin canal](/wiki/Canal_de_Saint-Quentin "Canal de Saint-Quentin"), between [Chauny](/wiki/Chauny "Chauny") on the [Oise](/wiki/Oise_(river) "Oise (river)") and Cambrai, was taken up in 1802 on the order of [Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") and completed in 1810, after the drilling of the [Riqueval Tunnel](/wiki/Riqueval_Tunnel "Riqueval Tunnel"). The canal and tunnel were opened with great pomp on 28 April 1810 by the Emperor and Empress [Marie-Louise](/wiki/Marie_Louise,_Duchess_of_Parma "Marie Louise, Duchess of Parma").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Saint-Quentin canal has experienced heavy traffic, but since 1966, the date of the opening of the [Canal du Nord](/wiki/Canal_du_Nord "Canal du Nord"), it has lost much of its importance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Waterways projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Waterways projects")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

A wide-gauge river link project, called [Seine-Escaut Link](/wiki/Seine%E2%80%93Nord_Europe_Canal "Seine–Nord Europe Canal") project is part of the 30 priority projects of the future [trans-European transport network](/wiki/Trans-European_Transport_Networks "Trans-European Transport Networks"). The route of this project is through [Marquion](/wiki/Marquion "Marquion"), 12 kilometres (7.5 mi) west of Cambrai. A platform of activities is planned therein. The commissioning of the canal could take place around 2015.[[30]](#cite_note-56)

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Airports")]

Cambrai is in close proximity to two airfields: *Cambrai-Epinoy*, to the north-west, whose use was booked at the [Airbase 103](/w/index.php?title=Airbase_103_Cambrai-%C3%89pinoy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Airbase 103 Cambrai-Épinoy (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Base_a%C3%A9rienne_103_Cambrai-%C3%89pinoy "fr:Base aérienne 103 Cambrai-Épinoy")] until its closure in 2012, and *[Cambrai Niergnies](/wiki/Cambrai-Niergnies_Airport "Cambrai-Niergnies Airport")*, 5 kilometres (3.1 mi) to the southeast, opened to recreational aviation.

Within an approximate radius of 1 hr 30 mins by road are five major airports: [Lille-Lesquin](/wiki/Lille_Airport "Lille Airport") at 60 kilometres (37 mi), [Brussels South Charleroi](/wiki/Brussels_South_Charleroi_Airport "Brussels South Charleroi Airport") at 114 kilometres (71 mi), [Brussels National](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport") at 148 kilometres (92 mi), [Paris Beauvais-Tillé](/wiki/Beauvais%E2%80%93Till%C3%A9_Airport "Beauvais–Tillé Airport") at 151 kilometres (94 mi) and [Paris Charles-de-Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_Airport "Charles de Gaulle Airport") at 152 kilometres (94 mi).

### Urban transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Urban transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/LL_67_-_CAMBRAI_-_La_Grand%27Place_-_Station_des_Tramways.JPG/250px-LL_67_-_CAMBRAI_-_La_Grand%27Place_-_Station_des_Tramways.JPG)](/wiki/File:LL_67_-_CAMBRAI_-_La_Grand%27Place_-_Station_des_Tramways.JPG)

The tramway in 1905, in the main square

By 1897, that is, upon completion of the demolition of the fortifications, the city had considered the construction of electric [tram](/wiki/Tram "Tram") lines. It was a very modern solution for the time, since electric traction only appeared in 1881 and the development of this mode of transport didn't truly occur until 1895 in Paris and the Paris region. In 1903, the network of the [Compagnie des tramways de Cambrai](/w/index.php?title=Tramway_of_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tramway of Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tramway_de_Cambrai "fr:Tramway de Cambrai")] was opened, 16 kilometres (9.9 mi)[[b 20]](#cite_note-57) and with five lines.[[31]](#cite_note-58) After World War I, the uneconomic network was not recommissioned.[[a 4]](#cite_note-59)

Since 1933 the agglomeration of Cambrai has been served by a [network of buses](/w/index.php?title=Public_transport_of_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Public transport of Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transports_en_commun_de_Cambrai "fr:Transports en commun de Cambrai")], five urban routes were operating in 2010, run by CFC (Railways of Cambrésis):

* A: Luxembourg – Cambrai stations – [Escaudœuvres](/wiki/Escaud%C5%93uvres "Escaudœuvres")
* B: [Neuville-Saint-Rémy](/wiki/Neuville-Saint-R%C3%A9my "Neuville-Saint-Rémy") – Cambrai stations – ZAC Cambrai-South
* C: Les Martigues-Pyrénées – Cambrai stations – [Raillencourt-Sainte-Olle](/wiki/Raillencourt-Sainte-Olle "Raillencourt-Sainte-Olle")
* D: Cambrai stations – Raillencourt-Sainte-Olle
* E: ZAC Cambrai-South – Cambrai stations

Since 7 January 2013, the agglomeration community of Cambrai has implemented a free shuttle which serves seventeen stops around the city.[[32]](#cite_note-60)

In 2008, the "Urban Transport Perimeter" (PTU) of Cambrai which, with 59,326 inhabitants, is the smallest of the twelve PTU of the [Nord-Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Nord-Pas-de-Calais "Nord-Pas-de-Calais") region had completed about 400,000 kilometres (250,000 mi) per year, corresponding to 740,000 journeys.[[33]](#cite_note-61)

The agglomeration community of Cambrai is the organising authority of urban transport. However, while it has twenty-three communes, only six were served by urban transport in 2006. For other towns and cities, the urban community has delegated jurisdiction to the department.[[34]](#cite_note-62)

In a relatively sparsely populated agglomeration with little extensive public transport, it cannot easily compete with the automobile. Nevertheless, the agglomeration community of Cambrai reflects on the future of urban transport from a perspective of [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development"), with aims to strengthen the supply of public transport in order to capture a portion of travel by private car, and reducing car traffic and CO2 emissions. The realisation of the new [multimodal transport](/wiki/Multimodal_transport "Multimodal transport") hub in the quarter of the railway station of Cambrai is part of this policy.[[35]](#cite_note-63)

## Urbanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Urbanism")]

### Urban morphology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Urban morphology")]

#### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Origins")]

The old centre of Cambrai was established on a small rise overlooking, from the right bank, the marshy area of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") Valley. Indications suggest that a [castrum](/wiki/Castra "Castra") was built at this location, although the scarcity of excavations at Cambrai in has provided no archaeological evidence.[[b 21]](#cite_note-64)[[c 2]](#cite_note-65) With its area of 4.4 hectares (11 acres), the very modest castrum would've been much smaller than those of [Boulogne](/wiki/Boulogne-sur-Mer "Boulogne-sur-Mer") at 13 hectares (32 acres) and [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims") at 56 hectares (140 acres), for example.

#### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Development")]

[Faubourgs](/wiki/Faubourg "Faubourg") were developed, at the time of the [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty") prosperity, to the north and west of the primitive castrum, around the churches of Saint-Vaast and Saint-Aubert. The looting of the city by the [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") in December 880 convinced the Bishop Dodilon to strengthen and expand fortifications: The new enclosure he built tripled the size of the city.[[b 22]](#cite_note-66) To the southeast, on a hillock called Mont-des-Bœufs, Bishop [Gaugericus](/wiki/Gaugericus "Gaugericus") had founded an abbey in 595, originally dedicated to Saint Médard and Saint Loup, then, after the death of the founder, Gaugericus himself. This abbey was certainly also protected by an enclosure. The space which separated these two urban cores hosted markets and fairs.[[b 23]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Cambrai_-_Cameracvm_vulgo_Cambray_-_Kamerijk_%28Atlas_van_Loon%29.jpg/250px-Cambrai_-_Cameracvm_vulgo_Cambray_-_Kamerijk_%28Atlas_van_Loon%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambrai_-_Cameracvm_vulgo_Cambray_-_Kamerijk_(Atlas_van_Loon).jpg)

City map dating from 1649

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Map_of_Cambrai_1710.jpg/250px-Map_of_Cambrai_1710.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Cambrai_1710.jpg)

Map of 1710

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/H%C3%B4pital_militaire_-Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles_%C3%A0_Cambrai_par_Augustin_Boutique.jpg/250px-H%C3%B4pital_militaire_-Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles_%C3%A0_Cambrai_par_Augustin_Boutique.jpg)](/wiki/File:H%C3%B4pital_militaire_-Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles_%C3%A0_Cambrai_par_Augustin_Boutique.jpg)

Chateau de Selles late 19th century

These two quarters were joined in the 11th century when Bishop [Gérard I](/wiki/Gerard_of_Florennes "Gerard of Florennes") built the churches of Saint Nicolas and the [Saint Sepulchre](/wiki/Cambrai_Cathedral "Cambrai Cathedral"), southeast of the city and east of Mont-des-Bœufs. The Hôtel de Ville (town hall), the market, butcher and various bodies of craftsmen settled in this new urban space that Bishop Lietbert had protected by an earth rampart. Even today the *Grand-place*, the covered market, and a few names of streets (the *Rue de Liniers*, *Rue des Rôtisseurs*, *Rue des Chaudronniers* and *Rue des Cordiers*) recall this stage of urban development.[[b 24]](#cite_note-68)

The Bishop [Gérard II](/wiki/Gerard_II_(bishop_of_Cambrai) "Gerard II (bishop of Cambrai)") later replaced the earth rampart with a stone wall with towers, gates and ditches and encompassed the entire built space. Therefore Cambrai had reached the perimeter it would retain until the 19th century: While other cities in the region such as [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") or [Douai](/wiki/Douai "Douai") expanded their enclosures until the 14th century, that of Cambrai was redesigned and reinforced, but without affecting the outline.[[b 25]](#cite_note-69)[[d 3]](#cite_note-70) The outline of this wall from the 11th century is still visible in the current boulevards.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles_de_Cambrai_01.jpg/250px-Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles_de_Cambrai_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles_de_Cambrai_01.jpg)

Selles Castle

It was probably under the episcopates of the bishops Gérard I, Liebert and Gérard II, in the 11th century, that was built the [Château de Selles](/w/index.php?title=Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles&action=edit&redlink=1 "Château de Selles (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles "fr:Château de Selles")], a fortress located on the edge of the Scheldt to the northwest of the city. In the 13th century, the Bishop Nicolas III de Fontaines ordered work to put it "on a good foot of defence".[[36]](#cite_note-71) This castle, owned by the bishop-counts, was intended as much to monitor the city as to ensure the defence. Its military role ended in the 16th century, when [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") grabbed the city and ordered the construction, on Mont-des-Bœufs northeast of the town, of a citadel for which 800 houses were demolished along with the Abbey of Saint Gaugericus.[[b 26]](#cite_note-72) The Château de Selles was then used as a prison.

#### Dismantling of fortifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Dismantling of fortifications")]

In the 19th century, the city was cramped within its fortified walls, which limited its development and prohibited any urban planning.

> Cambrai is one of the most inaccessible cities imaginable; its fortifications have a formidable appearance when it follows covered paths narrow and tortuous, crossing ditches filled by the Scheldt. Walls of extraordinary height enclose. The entrances, similar to tunnels, and barely sufficient for a car, lead to the city. All this overseen by a very daunting citadel built on top of Mont Saint-Géry.

— [Ardouin-Dumazet](/w/index.php?title=Victor-Eug%C3%A8ne_Ardouin-Dumazet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Victor-Eugène Ardouin-Dumazet (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victor-Eug%C3%A8ne_Ardouin-Dumazet "fr:Victor-Eugène Ardouin-Dumazet")], written in 1890.[[a 5]](#cite_note-73)

The dismantling of the fortifications, requested by petition as early as 1862, was finally accepted by the State after a further 30 years.[[b 27]](#cite_note-74) The work lasted 6 years and transformed the appearance of the city by the construction of a belt of wide [boulevards](/wiki/Boulevard "Boulevard"), the sale of new land to build, connecting the city to its suburbs and the establishment of public gardens.

#### Drainage work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Drainage work")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Cambrai_CPA_porte_des_arquets_escaut.jpg/250px-Cambrai_CPA_porte_des_arquets_escaut.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambrai_CPA_porte_des_arquets_escaut.jpg)

Entry of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") into the city through the gate of Arquets (postcard from the early 20th century)

Cambrai is built on the edge of the wide marshy area of the Scheldt Valley encircling the western part of the city, the [Château de Selles](/w/index.php?title=Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles&action=edit&redlink=1 "Château de Selles (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles "fr:Château de Selles")] to the north at the gate of the Holy Sepulchre in the south: Wet gardens, ponds, meadows, ponds and marsh formed a flood zone sometimes also used for the defence of the city. The suburb of Cantimpré, linking the ancient heart to the Scheldt below to the west, had raised several metres.[[a 6]](#cite_note-75) The two arms of the Scheldt (Escaut), the Escautins, are separated before entering the medieval town: The Escauette and the Clicotiau, which bathed the walls of the old urban core. These streams were probably due to man's hand because they do not correspond to natural landforms. However, it is unknown whether their origin dates back to the Roman era or is from a later time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Throughout the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), and again in modern times, the Scheldt and its arms required constant work: Repair of levees, enhancement of pavement, straightening of the bed, as well as the digging of ditches to regulate the course of the river, prevent floods and ensure as far as possible a steady water level, on which the mills and tanneries depended. Despite these works, floods were frequent.[[a 7]](#cite_note-76) The absorption of rainwater and household water descending from the upper areas of the city was also a problem. Bouly spoke in 1842, in his *History of Cambrai and le Cambrésis*, of "fast torrents [stormwater] formed today by rolling up the Scheldt".[[37]](#cite_note-77) In the Middle Ages the "flow of el kayère" (or "flow of the chair"),[[note 2]](#cite_note-78) close to the current *Grand-Place*, was dug a reservoir for "water courses of the falling waters of the sky", i.e. to control the flow of rainwater. In the 19th century aqueducts were built to carry these waters, and the streets were paved. Furthermore, in 1926 a drainage sewer was built at *Rue Blériot*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The draining of the wetlands that surround the town began in 1804. The work ended in 1951 with the drying of the small stream of Saint Benoît near the Liberty Stadium, itself built on this wetland which dominated the ancient city walls, and in 1953 with the covering of the Clicotiau.[[a 3]](#cite_note-p.62-44)

#### The 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: The 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Cambrai_-_Place_du_9_octobre.jpg/250px-Cambrai_-_Place_du_9_octobre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambrai_-_Place_du_9_octobre.jpg)

The reconstruction imposes a regional style

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/MERESSE_2038_-_CAMBRAI_Pendant_l%27incendie_-_La_Choque_et_Place_au_Bois.JPG/250px-MERESSE_2038_-_CAMBRAI_Pendant_l%27incendie_-_La_Choque_et_Place_au_Bois.JPG)](/wiki/File:MERESSE_2038_-_CAMBRAI_Pendant_l%27incendie_-_La_Choque_et_Place_au_Bois.JPG)

Ruins of the town centre, in 1917

The destruction caused by [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") necessitated a reconstruction of the centre. The municipality organised a competition, which was won by [Pierre Leprince-Ringuet](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_Leprince-Ringuet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre Leprince-Ringuet (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierre_Leprince-Ringuet "fr:Pierre Leprince-Ringuet")]. His project, inspired with both a [regionalist](/wiki/Regionalism_(politics) "Regionalism (politics)") taste and the [Hausmannian](/wiki/Georges-Eug%C3%A8ne_Haussmann "Georges-Eugène Haussmann") style, completely redesigned the layout of roads from the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). It was rebuilt as a modern city. The architect [Louis Marie Cordonnier](/wiki/Louis_Marie_Cordonnier "Louis Marie Cordonnier") described the prospects to elected representatives:

> The centre of the city is completely razed and destroyed. So, you have free rein. Your duty is to remake a city pleasant, modern, retaining its character. Acting otherwise would be a crime for which you would be responsible to future generations. Your streets were narrow, twisty: Straighten them, adjust them, expand them. Your houses were awkward, obscure, unhealthy: Enforce in your specifications the requirements for hygiene, for ventilation, for lighting.

— [Louis Marie Cordonnier](/wiki/Louis_Marie_Cordonnier "Louis Marie Cordonnier"), *La Vie des Cambrésiens* [The Life of the People of Cambrai][[a 8]](#cite_note-79)

The Leprince-Ringuet plan was only partially achieved, but the streets were removed, others expanded, and new paths were created as the *Avenue de la Victoire* [Avenue of Victory].[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Further destruction due to the [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") bombing of April–August 1944 again required a reconstruction. It was especially, in the years following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), to rehouse the victims and to cope with the expanding population. Priority was given to detached houses, and new quarters appeared, such as the "Martin–Martine" subdivision south-east of the city. In the 1980s, the municipality worked to restore and develop the ancient heritage.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Housing")]

Cambrai had 18,112 housing units in 2017, of which 15,925 were occupied as primary residences.[[38]](#cite_note-complet-80) The proportion of individual housing is 54.2%. [Primary residences](/wiki/Primary_residence "Primary residence") dominate with 87.9%, and one can especially note the very low proportion of [secondary residences](/wiki/Holiday_cottage "Holiday cottage") in Cambrai: 1.0%.[[38]](#cite_note-complet-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Lotissement_Martin_Martine_ann%C3%A9es_1970.JPG/250px-Lotissement_Martin_Martine_ann%C3%A9es_1970.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lotissement_Martin_Martine_ann%C3%A9es_1970.JPG)

A street of houses in the subdivision "Martin Martine" constructed by the *Maison Familiale* group, characteristic habitat of the years 1960–1970

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Cambrai_-_Rue_Vaucelette.jpg/250px-Cambrai_-_Rue_Vaucelette.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambrai_-_Rue_Vaucelette.jpg)

The old Cambrai

The proportion of house owners, at 44.3%, is lower than in the rest of the country (57.5%). The tenants are much more numerous, 54.1% in Cambrai, against little more than 40% in France. Among rental housing, the proportion of [HLM](/wiki/HLM "HLM") housing, at 16.7%, is significantly higher than the national average of 14.8%.[[38]](#cite_note-complet-80)

The age of the accommodation in Cambrai is distinguished from both regional and national averages. Housing is older, on average, in Cambrai than in the rest of the country. The proportion of (pre-1945) "old" housing, is 33.5%, which is significantly higher than the French average of 22.2%. Meanwhile, dwellings built between 1946 and 1970 account for 37.0% of the total in Cambrai, significantly above the 21.5% of the country. This proportion can probably be explained by the necessary reconstruction which followed the destruction of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), as well as programmes of Maison Familiale group homes in the 1960s and 1970s. More recent housing is relatively less in the city than in the rest of France or even in the region: 7.3% of accommodation in Cambrai was built between 1991 and 2005, compared with 16.1% in France; 5.2% have been built since 2006, compared with 10.9% in France.[[38]](#cite_note-complet-80) This is probably a consequence of the less dynamic economy and population of the city in recent years.

The agglomeration community seeks to improve the quality of the accommodation by various means: Encourage rehabilitation of vacant properties, expand available accommodation which is suitable for elderly or handicapped people, increase the supply of rentals and promote the construction of more energy-efficient housing. The creation of 960 new social houses was provided over six years, from 2008 to 2014.[[39]](#cite_note-CAChabitat-81)

The [Urban social cohesion contract](/w/index.php?title=Urban_social_cohesion_contract&action=edit&redlink=1 "Urban social cohesion contract (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contrat_urbain_de_coh%C3%A9sion_sociale "fr:Contrat urbain de cohésion sociale")] signed in 2007 with the city and the urban community was the first of the Nord department. Five quarters, not classified as "sensitive urban areas", are concerned: The old centre where old habitat remains, the *l'Amérique* and *La Forêt* estates, consisting primarily of multi-family housing, and the *d'Esnes* and *de Guise* estates dominated by single-family housing.[[39]](#cite_note-CAChabitat-81)
