Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(December 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Porte_Notre-Dame_%2812532%29.jpg/250px-Porte_Notre-Dame_%2812532%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porte_Notre-Dame_(12532).jpg)

The Notre-Dame Gate (1634)

A large part of the monumental heritage of Cambrai has disappeared over the centuries. It was firstly [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor"), in order to build a citadel at the Mont-des-Bœufs, who ordered the destruction of the Abbey Saint-Gery of Gothic style in 1543.

During the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") all of the religious buildings of the town were sold as national property and destroyed, including the [old cathedral](/wiki/Old_Cambrai_Cathedral "Old Cambrai Cathedral"). Only four churches, a converted attic, a hospital, a temple of reason and a prison, were spared.

The dismantling of the fortifications, from 1894, led to the disappearance of many [gates](/wiki/City_gate "City gate"). Some have been preserved thanks to the interventions of the Society of Emulation of the city.

[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") was again responsible for significant destruction, the German army having undermined and torched the city centre before retreating in September 1918. A total of 1,214 buildings were destroyed, including the city hall, which was rebuilt in the [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") style before the Revolution by the architects [Jacques Denis Antoine](/wiki/Jacques_Denis_Antoine "Jacques Denis Antoine") and [Nicolas-Henri Jardin](/wiki/Nicolas-Henri_Jardin "Nicolas-Henri Jardin").[[a 10]](#cite_note-153)

Finally at the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), in April 1944, and then again in May, July and until 11 August, Cambrai suffered Allied bombardments. A total of 55% of the buildings were heavily damaged and 13% were completely destroyed.

Despite this considerable destruction, the city kept an important monumental heritage. Cambrai has been classified as a [City of Art and History](/wiki/French_Towns_and_Lands_of_Art_and_History "French Towns and Lands of Art and History") since 1992, the first town of the Nord department to obtain this prestigious label.

#### Religious heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Religious heritage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Cath%C3%A9drale_Notre-Dame-de-Gr%C3%A2ce_de_Cambrai.jpg/250px-Cath%C3%A9drale_Notre-Dame-de-Gr%C3%A2ce_de_Cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cath%C3%A9drale_Notre-Dame-de-Gr%C3%A2ce_de_Cambrai.jpg)

The Cathedral of Our Lady

The [Our Lady of Grace Cathedral](/wiki/Cambrai_Cathedral "Cambrai Cathedral") was completed in 1703, in the classical style of the time, as the abbey church of the Holy Sepulchre. The church survived the turbulence of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") as a Temple of Reason from 1794. The admirable [Gothic Cathedral](/wiki/Old_Cambrai_Cathedral "Old Cambrai Cathedral") from the 12th century was destroyed in the aftermath the Revolution of 1789. There is no trace on the present *Place Fénelon* of the former building. Bishop Louis Belmas adopted the former abbey church as the new cathedral in 1801.[[103]](#cite_note-154)

The apse contains the monumental tomb of Fénelon, a masterpiece of the sculptor [David d'Angers](/wiki/David_d%27Angers "David d'Angers"), and the semitransepts with *l'Icône Notre Dame de Grâce* [the Icon of Our Lady of Grace] and the nine reputed [grisailles](/wiki/Grisaille "Grisaille") by [Geeraerts](/wiki/Marten_Jozef_Geeraerts "Marten Jozef Geeraerts") of Antwerp. The grand [organs](/wiki/Organ_(music)#Chamber_organ "Organ (music)") were built by the house of [Pierre Schyven](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_Schyven&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre Schyven (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierre_Schyven "fr:Pierre Schyven")] of Ixelles in 1897. After the events of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), extensive restoration was undertaken by the organ builder Auguste Convers, who brought the current instrument to 49 stops with 3,670 pipes. The building was classified in the inventory of [Historic Monuments](/wiki/Monument_historique "Monument historique") on 9 August 1906.[[104]](#cite_note-155)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/%C3%89glise_Saint-G%C3%A9ry_-_Cambrai_01.jpg/250px-%C3%89glise_Saint-G%C3%A9ry_-_Cambrai_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%89glise_Saint-G%C3%A9ry_-_Cambrai_01.jpg)

Saint-Géry

The [Grand Seminary Chapel](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Seminary_Chapel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Grand Seminary Chapel (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chapelle_des_J%C3%A9suites_de_Cambrai "fr:Chapelle des Jésuites de Cambrai")] most commonly called the College of the Jesuits' Chapel, completed in 1692, is a unique example of [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") art in France, to the north of Paris. The chapel served as a prison to the nearby Revolutionary Court in 1794, and it was classified in the inventory of Historic Monuments on 30 April 1920.[[105]](#cite_note-156)

The [Church of Saint-Géry](/w/index.php?title=%C3%89glise_Saint-G%C3%A9ry_de_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Église Saint-Géry de Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89glise_Saint-G%C3%A9ry_de_Cambrai "fr:Église Saint-Géry de Cambrai")], a listed historical monument since 26 November 1919,[[106]](#cite_note-157) is one of the oldest monuments of Cambrai. It contains a remarkable [choir screen](/wiki/Rood_screen "Rood screen") in polychromatic marble carved by the Cambrai native [Gaspard Marsy](/wiki/Gaspard_and_Balthazard_Marsy "Gaspard and Balthazard Marsy") as well as *La mise au tombeauu* by [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens") dating from 1616. The grand organs built in 1867 by [Merklin](/wiki/Joseph_Merklin "Joseph Merklin") were the subject of a significant transformation in 1978. The current instrument has 41 stops. This church has been the subject of a restoration of the frontage and roofing over a period of four years (2011–2015).

Other buildings of Cambrai are also classified or listed as Historic Monuments. The former [Convent of the Recollects](/w/index.php?title=Convent_of_the_Recollects_of_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Convent of the Recollects of Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Couvent_des_R%C3%A9collets_de_Cambrai "fr:Couvent des Récollets de Cambrai")] has been registered since 2 March 1943,[[107]](#cite_note-158) and the [Béguinages Saint-Vaast and Saint-Nicolas](/w/index.php?title=B%C3%A9guinages_Saint-Vaast_and_Saint-Nicolas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Béguinages Saint-Vaast and Saint-Nicolas (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%A9guinages_Saint-Vaast_et_Saint-Nicolas "fr:Béguinages Saint-Vaast et Saint-Nicolas")] have been classified as Historic Monuments since 1949.[[108]](#cite_note-159)

#### Military heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Military heritage")]

The Citadel: Despite its dismantling in the 19th century, the Citadel of Charles V retained the counter-mine galleries which are today buried; the Royal gate and drawbridge, classified in the inventory of historical monuments on 14 April 1932,[[109]](#cite_note-160) flanked on the back of two guardhouses and an arsenal of the 16th century. Among subsequent developments, a powder magazine, housing for officers and a "bombproof" barracks of the 19th century are also noteworthy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Château de Selles](/w/index.php?title=Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles&action=edit&redlink=1 "Château de Selles (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Selles "fr:Château de Selles")] is an old fortified château, which was built in the 11th century. Once isolated by the waters of the Scheldt, it has retained its towers and walls and especially buried ducts. The ducts include much graffiti which attests to the desperation of the prisoners, confined on the orders of the Count-Bishop.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Portes de Paris](/w/index.php?title=Porte_de_Paris_(Cambrai)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Porte de Paris (Cambrai) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porte_de_Paris_(Cambrai) "fr:Porte de Paris (Cambrai)")] (late 14th century),[[110]](#cite_note-161) [Notre Dame](/w/index.php?title=Porte_Notre-Dame_(Cambrai)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Porte Notre-Dame (Cambrai) (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porte_Notre-Dame_(Cambrai) "fr:Porte Notre-Dame (Cambrai)")] (17th century)[[111]](#cite_note-162) and the [Tours des Sottes](/w/index.php?title=Tour_Saint-Fiacre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tour Saint-Fiacre (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tour_Saint-Fiacre "fr:Tour Saint-Fiacre")] (or Saint-Fiacre),[[112]](#cite_note-163) the Caudron (1st half of the 15th century)[[113]](#cite_note-164) and [Arquets](/w/index.php?title=Tour_des_Arquets&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tour des Arquets (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tour_des_Arquets "fr:Tour des Arquets")] (16th century)[[114]](#cite_note-165) are the remains of the medieval walls.

#### Civil heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Civil heritage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/H%C3%B4tel_de_ville_de_Cambrai_%2897619%29.jpg/250px-H%C3%B4tel_de_ville_de_Cambrai_%2897619%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:H%C3%B4tel_de_ville_de_Cambrai_(97619).jpg)

Martin and Martine strike the hours in the bell tower of the Hôtel de Ville (town hall)

The [Belfry of Cambrai](/w/index.php?title=Belfry_of_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belfry of Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beffroi_de_Cambrai "fr:Beffroi de Cambrai")], formerly the bell tower of the Church of Saint-Martin. The monument, built in the 15th century, became a belfry of Cambrai in 1550. Classified in the inventory of historical monuments on 15 July 1965,[[115]](#cite_note-166) in 2005 the belfry was inscribed on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List") as part of the [Belfries of Belgium and France](/wiki/Belfries_of_Belgium_and_France "Belfries of Belgium and France") site, in recognition of its architecture and importance to the history of municipal power in Europe.[[116]](#cite_note-unesco-167)

The city hall, renovated in 1932, opens onto the *Grand'Place* by a majestic Greek-style façade, surmounted by a bell tower where two bronze bell ringers, giant and Moorish type, strike the hours on a big bell above the big clock: [Martin and Martine](/w/index.php?title=Martin_and_Martine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Martin and Martine (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_et_Martine "fr:Martin et Martine")], the protectors of the city. The marriage hall contains a series of frescoes and can be visited on request.

The Hotel de Francqueville (18th century) houses the rich collections of the [Museum of Cambrai](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Museum of Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_Cambrai "fr:Musée de Cambrai")], considerably enlarged and renovated in 1994.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The relief map of the city, as it was at the end of the 17th century, is the starting point for essential guided tours of the city.

The *Maison Espagnole* [Spanish House], headquarters of the Tourist Office, dates from 1595 and is the last house which is half-timbered and gabled on regional-style street. Oak sculptures ([chimeras](/wiki/Chimera_(mythology) "Chimera (mythology)") and [caryatids](/wiki/Caryatid "Caryatid")) which adorned its façade in the 19th century are exposed on the first floor inside after undergoing a serious restoration. One can visit its medieval cellars. This building has been classified in the inventory of historical monuments on 31 August 1920.[[117]](#cite_note-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/March%C3%A9_couvert_de_Cambrai_02.jpg/250px-March%C3%A9_couvert_de_Cambrai_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:March%C3%A9_couvert_de_Cambrai_02.jpg)

The covered market

The covered market, built after World War II, is home to lively Les Halles market days.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The subterranean space[[a 1]](#cite_note-p.37-40) which extends below the centre of the city, as in other medieval cities, was explored in the middle of the 19th century as well as to the end of the 20th century. Carved into the white chalk, it includes galleries and Romanesque and Gothic vaulted rooms. There are also wells and niches for statues. These excavations have served as quarries for the extraction of construction materials, as well as stone for lime: The ["catiches"](/w/index.php?title=Catiche&action=edit&redlink=1 "Catiche (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catiche "fr:Catiche")] [underground quarries] are inverted funnels about 15 metres (49 ft) deep and 10–12 metres (33–39 ft) wide at the base.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This underground space was also used, until 1944, for shelters and caches during sieges, invasion or bombing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Their dating is uncertain: It is not impossible that some of these excavations were carved during the Roman era, but it is likely that they were spaced out over a long period, according to the needs. Tours are organised by the tourist office.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Memorial heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Memorial heritage")]

The German military cemetery of the *Route de Solesmes* and Cambrai East Military Cemetery: The cemetery and the *Route de Solesmes* was created by the German army in March 1917. Occupied since 1914, Cambrai was an important centre of command, logistics and health for the occupant.[[118]](#cite_note-169) The cemetery was opened to accommodate the bodies of soldiers who died in hospitals in the city, including at the end of the [Battle of Arras](/wiki/Battle_of_Arras_(1917) "Battle of Arras (1917)") (April–May 1917) and the [Battle of Cambrai](/wiki/Battle_of_Cambrai_(1917) "Battle of Cambrai (1917)") (November–December 1917). The cemetery currently has 10,685 German graves, as well as those of 192 Russian prisoners of war and six Romanians.[[119]](#cite_note-170) Two spaces, forming the Cambrai East Military Cemetery, contain the graves of 501 soldiers of the British Imperial Army.[[120]](#cite_note-171)

### Environmental heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Environmental heritage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Kiosque_%C3%A0_musique_du_Jardin_Public_de_Cambrai.jpg/250px-Kiosque_%C3%A0_musique_du_Jardin_Public_de_Cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kiosque_%C3%A0_musique_du_Jardin_Public_de_Cambrai.jpg)

The bandstand is one of the oldest in France

Cambrai has the label "Floral City" with three flowers awarded by the *National Council of Floral Cities and Villages of France* in the [Floral Competition of Cities and Villages](/wiki/Concours_des_villes_et_villages_fleuris "Concours des villes et villages fleuris").[[121]](#cite_note-172)

The current public garden dates from the 19th century, which saw the creation of green spaces in the middle to encourage [hygienics](/wiki/Hygiene "Hygiene") and which were liberated in addition to the areas occupied by the fortifications. This garden, divided into three distinct but contiguous parts, is located on the site of the old fortifications that surrounded the citadel built under [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor"):

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Grottes_Jardin_public_Cambrai.JPG/250px-Grottes_Jardin_public_Cambrai.JPG)](/wiki/File:Grottes_Jardin_public_Cambrai.JPG)

The "caves" of the public garden

* The "flower garden", designed by the landscape architect [Jean-Pierre Barillet-Deschamps](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Barillet-Deschamps "Jean-Pierre Barillet-Deschamps"), was established between 1852 and 1865 on 6 hectares (15 acres). This garden was originally the only one planned but, at the insistence of the prefect of the time, it was increased to 9 hectares (22 acres) in 1864.[[a 11]](#cite_note-173)
* The "Monstrelet Garden" designed "in English", was soon added to the previous. It is so called because it houses a statue of [Enguerrand de Monstrelet](/wiki/Enguerrand_de_Monstrelet "Enguerrand de Monstrelet"), chronicler of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") who was the [Provost](/wiki/Pr%C3%A9v%C3%B4t "Prévôt") of Cambrai. In 1876, the bandstand was erected, built on the plans of André de Baralle.
* The "garden of caves", built in the early 19th century, carries a total area of more than 15 hectares (37 acres). The "caves", decorated with a waterfall, were the main attraction and gave their name to this part of the garden. They were completed in 1906 and in 2010 are awaiting rehabilitation.

These gardens, and in particular their statues, were damaged by the two world wars. In 1972, a modern hall, named as the *Palais des Grottes* [Mansion of Caves] and hosting exhibitions, trade fairs and concerts, was built in the middle of the garden of the same name.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Canard_v%C3%A9g%C3%A9tal_sur_un_rond-point_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg/120px-Canard_v%C3%A9g%C3%A9tal_sur_un_rond-point_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canard_v%C3%A9g%C3%A9tal_sur_un_rond-point_%C3%A0_Cambrai.jpg)

Plant swan on a roundabout

A few other squares or gardens complement the green spaces of Cambrai: Fenelon Square, established in 1861 to the designs of Barillet-Deschamps on the site of the [ancient metropolis](/wiki/Old_Cambrai_Cathedral "Old Cambrai Cathedral") and decorated in 1864 with a water fountain, that of the *Place Marcelin Berthelot*, which dates from 1911. This is at the foot of the walls of the Château de Selles, dating from the same year. That beside the Arquets tower dates from 1954.[[c 3]](#cite_note-174) The avenues and boulevards planted with trees and flowers to complete make Cambrai a "green" city.

### Cultural heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: Cultural heritage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Cambrai.jpg/250px-Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Cambrai.jpg)

The theatre of Cambrai, between the National Conservatory of Music and Dramatic Art and the chapel of the old Saint-Julien Hospital

The [Musée des Beaux-Arts de Cambrai](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_des_Beaux-Arts_de_Cambrai "Musée des Beaux-Arts de Cambrai") opened in 1847 to present revolutionary seizures, has been installed since 1893 in a mansion of the 18th century, the Hotel de Francqueville. Renovated in 1994, it has three departments (archaeology, fine arts and heritage of Cambrai) on 4,300 square metres (46,000 sq ft) of which the most important is that of art. It presents Flemish and Dutch paintings from the 17th century and French artists of the 19th and 20th century. A recent donation also allowed it to present a collection of geometric [abstractions](/wiki/Abstraction_(art) "Abstraction (art)") of the second half of the 20th century.[[122]](#cite_note-175)

The Diocesan Museum of Sacred Art still officially retains its label "[Musée de France](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_France "Musée de France")",[[note 3]](#cite_note-176) although it was closed to the public in 1975. This private museum is managed by the diocese, which is looking for ways to reopen the collections to the public. The items of this collection may be the subject of loans for temporary exhibitions and include objects from the archaeological excavations of the city of Cambrai, architectural elements, sculpture, paintings, pieces of jewellery and liturgical ornaments.[[123]](#cite_note-177) This museum's history began in 1926 when Monseignor [Chollet](/w/index.php?title=Jean-Arthur_Chollet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean-Arthur Chollet (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean-Arthur_Chollet "fr:Jean-Arthur Chollet")], Archbishop of Cambrai, established a commission of religious history and sacred art aimed to inventory and preserve the archives and movable heritage of the diocese. Canon Cyrille Thelliez became secretary. In 1958, many religious objects from the diocese were gathered together and Thelliez founded the Diocesan Museum, the first religious art museum opened in France. The museum was installed in the former chapel of the Grand Seminary.[[124]](#cite_note-178)

The Théâtre de Cambrai was built in 1924 by the architect [Pierre Leprince-Ringuet](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_Leprince-Ringuet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre Leprince-Ringuet (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierre_Leprince-Ringuet "fr:Pierre Leprince-Ringuet")], on the site of a chapel of the 16th century which was destroyed during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The chapel had been abandoned for 25 years when its rehabilitation was undertaken in 1999. The renovated theatre was inaugurated in 2003. It is an Italian theatre of 700 seats which hosts various performances, including those of the *Scènes mitoyennes* [Adjoining Stages] association and the [Juventus classical music festival](/w/index.php?title=Festival_Juventus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Festival Juventus (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Festival_Juventus "fr:Festival Juventus")].

The *Palais des grottes* [Mansion of Caves] situated in the public garden, is a large multi-purpose hall with a capacity of 1,500 people and which can accommodate concerts, (including the [BetiZFest](/w/index.php?title=BetiZFest&action=edit&redlink=1 "BetiZFest (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/BetiZFest "fr:BetiZFest")]), fairs or exhibitions. Its remarkable roof shape, formed of a [hyperbolic paraboloid](/wiki/Paraboloid "Paraboloid") (or "saddle"), was built in 1974 by then-advanced techniques and demonstrates a concrete form of architecture in the 20th century.[[a 12]](#cite_note-179)

The media library is a classified municipal library:[[125]](#cite_note-180) It has an important old [fonds](/wiki/Fonds "Fonds"), with 956 manuscripts, the oldest dating back to the 7th century, from the confiscations made during the revolutionary era to religious communities, very important in the city, and emigrants of the region. This fonds was subsequently enriched by gifts, bequests and purchases.[[126]](#cite_note-181) In 1975, it was one of the first institutions to adopt the title of "media library". It is divided into four services: Youth, adults, library and local history and old books.

Cambrai has a national school of music and dramatic arts[[127]](#cite_note-182) which obtained the *Conservatoire à rayonnement départemental* label in 2007.

### Culinary specialities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Culinary specialities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/B%C3%AAtises_de_Cambrai.jpg/250px-B%C3%AAtises_de_Cambrai.jpg)](/wiki/File:B%C3%AAtises_de_Cambrai.jpg)

Bêtises de Cambrai

The two best-known gastronomic specialties of Cambrai are the [Andouillette de Cambrai](/w/index.php?title=Andouillette_de_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andouillette de Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andouillette_de_Cambrai "fr:Andouillette de Cambrai")], a sausage traditionally made of ground veal (which was prohibited by European regulations, following the episode of [mad cow disease](/wiki/Bovine_spongiform_encephalopathy "Bovine spongiform encephalopathy"), [until 2015](https://www.weekend-esprithautsdefrance.com/la-gastronomie-des-hauts-de-france/andouillette-andouille)), which associated gastronomic society is one of the most representative in the region, and the [Bêtise de Cambrai](/wiki/B%C3%AAtise_de_Cambrai "Bêtise de Cambrai"), a coated mint confection which is one of the most emblematic gourmet specialties of France.

The gastronomy of Cambrai also accounts for other less known specialities: Tripe, liver pâté with plums, hare with grapes, [hochepot](/wiki/Hochepot "Hochepot") of [partridge](/wiki/Partridge "Partridge") with puréed lentils, but also the [Boulette de Cambrai](/wiki/Boulette_de_Cambrai "Boulette de Cambrai") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boulette_de_Cambrai "fr:Boulette de Cambrai")], [fromage blanc](/wiki/Fromage_blanc "Fromage blanc") with fine herbs, and also a cheese [trademarked](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") as ["Tome de Cambrai"](/w/index.php?title=Tome_de_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tome de Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tome_de_Cambrai "fr:Tome de Cambrai")], or even crackers and *pain crotté* [muddy bread] (a type of [French toast](/wiki/French_toast "French toast")).

### Heraldry, motto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: Heraldry, motto")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Arms of Cambrai](/wiki/File:Blason_cambrai.svg "Arms of Cambrai")  Arms of Cambrai | The arms of Cambrai are [blazoned](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon"): *"Or, a double-headed eagle sable, (haloed) beaked and membered gules, overall an inescutcheon Or, 3 lions azure."*  The first known arms appear on a seal from 1340. They represented an eagle holding in its talons the arms of le Cambrésis, *"of Or three Lion Cubs of azure"*. Under the Spanish domination these arms were slightly modified, the double-headed eagle of the Holy Roman Empire became an honourable figure[[note 4]](#cite_note-183) and arms of the Cambrésis being removed. The coat of arms thus means "Cambrai city of the empire and capital of the Cambrésis".[[128]](#cite_note-184) |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | The eagle is usually surmounted with the ducal crown, the city having been elevated to a Duchy in 1510. In 1815 Cambrai received from [Louis XVIII](/wiki/Louis_XVIII "Louis XVIII") the right again to its ancient arms. In 1919, it was awarded the cross of the [Legion of Honour](/wiki/Legion_of_Honour "Legion of Honour"), and in 1945 the [Croix de Guerre](/wiki/Croix_de_guerre_1939%E2%80%931945_(France) "Croix de guerre 1939–1945 (France)").  On the front of the Chamber of Commerce (shown adjoining), built after the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the arms are surmounted by the Ducal Crown, framed by the giants [Martin and Martine](/w/index.php?title=Martin_and_Martine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Martin and Martine (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_et_Martine "fr:Martin et Martine")], and increased by the cross of the Legion of Honour. |

The motto of the city has changed several times:

* 1579: *"Cambray, city of peace."*
* 1580: *"Concordia parvæ res crescunt"* ("Harmony makes small things grow"). This motto is the same as that of the [United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") (*"[Unity makes strength](/wiki/Unity_makes_strength "Unity makes strength")"*)
* Current motto: *"Proud of its past, sure of its future."*

The logo of the city is a stylisation of the steeples of the [cathedral](/wiki/Cambrai_Cathedral "Cambrai Cathedral"), the Saint-Géry Church and [belfry](/w/index.php?title=Belfry_of_Cambrai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belfry of Cambrai (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beffroi_de_Cambrai "fr:Beffroi de Cambrai")], a visual signature of the city, visible from afar. Locally, Cambrai is known as "the city of three spires".

### Military life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Military life")]

In 1711–1712, during the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"), the regiments of [Rousillon](/w/index.php?title=Roussillon_Regiment&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roussillon Regiment (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/R%C3%A9giment_de_Roussillon "fr:Régiment de Roussillon")] and [Royal-Comtois](/wiki/R%C3%A9giment_Royal-Comtois "Régiment Royal-Comtois") had their barracks at Cambrai.[[129]](#cite_note-185)

From the late 19th century, two military units were stationed at Cambrai. The [1st Infantry Regiment](/wiki/1st_Infantry_Regiment_(France) "1st Infantry Regiment (France)") was quartered at Cambrai from 1870 to 1914, when it left for Belgium, before returning in 1919. It was split between the citadel and the Renel barracks. In 1940 it was again sent to Belgium, before being dissolved in 1942 in the free zone and did not return to Cambrai. The [4th Cuirassier Regiment](/w/index.php?title=4th_Cuirassier_Regiment&action=edit&redlink=1 "4th Cuirassier Regiment (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/4e_r%C3%A9giment_de_cuirassiers_(France) "fr:4e régiment de cuirassiers (France)")] arrived in Cambrai in 1889 and is the Mortier quarter. This regiment was dissolved at the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the Mortier quarter was assigned to the Selection Centre No. 2, today disbanded.

Until 2012, the [Airbase 103](/w/index.php?title=Airbase_103_Cambrai-%C3%89pinoy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Airbase 103 Cambrai-Épinoy (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Base_a%C3%A9rienne_103_Cambrai-%C3%89pinoy "fr:Base aérienne 103 Cambrai-Épinoy")] "[René Mouchotte](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Mouchotte "René Mouchotte")" was near the city, founded in 1953 and which hosted the 01.012 Fighter Squadron "Cambrésis" created in 1952, the 02.012 "Picardy" and the 03.012 "Cornouailles" [Cornwall], as well as a ground-to-air defence squadron.

### Cambrai in literature and cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Cambrai in literature and cinema")]

* [Ernst Jünger](/wiki/Ernst_J%C3%BCnger "Ernst Jünger") in *[Storm of Steel](/wiki/Storm_of_Steel "Storm of Steel")*, dedicated to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), described Cambrai in 1917:

> Cambrai is a small, quiet and sleepy city of Artois (sic), to the name of which is attached many historical memories. Narrow and antiquated alleys run like a maze around the huge city hall, doors eaten away by the centuries and many churches, the largest of which saw Fénelon preach. Heavy steeples stand amongst a jumble of pointed gables. Broad avenues lead to a well-kept public garden, which is adorned with a monument to Louis Blériot.
>
> The inhabitants are peaceful and cordial people, who lead in their big houses, simple in appearance, but richly furnished, with a life of wellbeing. The small city has been rightfully nicknamed "the city of millionaires" because, just before the war, there were forty of these [Croesus](/wiki/Croesus "Croesus")-like people.
>
> The [Great War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") tore this hole of the province in its sleep of Sleeping Beauty and the gigantic turn of home struggles...

— [Ernst Jünger](/wiki/Ernst_J%C3%BCnger "Ernst Jünger"), *[Storm of Steel](/wiki/Storm_of_Steel "Storm of Steel")*[[130]](#cite_note-186)

* The streets of Cambrai served as a backdrop to *Sang noir* [Black Blood], a 90-minute fiction filmed from 7 March to 6 April 2006 through Production of [France 3](/wiki/France_3 "France 3") Lille, based on the novel of the same name by [Louis Guilloux](/wiki/Louis_Guilloux "Louis Guilloux") which takes place in 1917 in a small town, far from the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)").
* In 2007, the construction of the bypass of Cambrai was used for the filming of the movie *[In the Beginning](/wiki/In_the_Beginning_(2009_film) "In the Beginning (2009 film)")* by [Xavier Giannoli](/wiki/Xavier_Giannoli "Xavier Giannoli"). Some scenes of the film *Présumé coupable* [Presumed Guilty], by Vincent Garenq, were shot at Cambrai in April 2010.
* The comic book *[Asterix and the Banquet](/wiki/Asterix_and_the_Banquet "Asterix and the Banquet")* by [Goscinny](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Goscinny "René Goscinny") and [Uderzo](/wiki/Albert_Uderzo "Albert Uderzo") have a journey to various cities in Roman Gaul, including *Camaracum* (Cambrai): [Asterix](/wiki/Asterix_(character) "Asterix (character)") and [Obelix](/wiki/Obelix "Obelix") buy [bêtises](/wiki/B%C3%AAtise_de_Cambrai "Bêtise de Cambrai").
* The young-adult novel *Les Bêtises de Cambrai* (Airvey, 2011) by Eric Callens.

### Cambrai and philately

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cambrai&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: Cambrai and philately")]

Four postage stamps have been issued in connection with the city of Cambrai:

* 10 July 1947, a stamp commemorating [Fénelon](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_F%C3%A9nelon "François Fénelon"), Archbishop of Cambrai, with a face value of 4.50 [Francs](/wiki/French_franc "French franc").[[131]](#cite_note-187)
* 19 February 1972, a stamp commemorating [Louis Blériot](/wiki/Louis_Bl%C3%A9riot "Louis Blériot"), born in Cambrai, with a face value of 0.50 Francs with surcharge: 0.10 Francs for the benefit of the French Red Cross.[[132]](#cite_note-188)
* 14 May 1977, a stamp commemorating the capture of Cambrai in 1677 and the annexation of Cambrésis by France, with a face value of 0.80 Francs[[133]](#cite_note-189)
* 25 July 2009, a stamp commemorating the crossing of the English channel by [Louis Blériot](/wiki/Louis_Bl%C3%A9riot "Louis Blériot"), with a face value of €2.00.[[134]](#cite_note-190)
